methodsAttempted.notes: Firecrawl search mostly returned department manuals, WAC publication pages, and event pages rather than central research AI policy.
Verified official CSU subdomains used for evidence: ai.colostate.edu, datagov.csusystem.edu, tilt.colostate.edu, resolutioncenter.colostate.edu, and www.natsci.colostate.edu.

artifacts.summary: Official-source discovery found CSU AI tools guidance, CSU-GPT information, CSU System AI governance guidelines, TILT teaching and academic-integrity guidance, and Student Resolution Center academic misconduct definitions. Department, publication, bootcamp, and stale-PDF sources were rejected or kept out of central policy claims.

artifacts.claimText: CSU's AI Tools page lists CSU-GPT, Microsoft Copilot Chat with CSU NetID, and Microsoft Teams Premium as currently approved tools for handling sensitive CSU data.

citation.citationTitle: AI Tools | AI @ CSU | Colorado State University

artifacts.claimText: CSU's AI Tools page says other AI tools such as ChatGPT, Gemini, or Anthropic Claude may be used for non-sensitive public information only.

citation.citationTitle: AI Tools | AI @ CSU | Colorado State University

artifacts.claimText: CSU-GPT is described by CSU as operating inside CSU's Microsoft Azure environment, keeping data, prompts, and uploads inside the university's Microsoft Azure tenant.

citation.citationTitle: CSU-GPT | AI @ CSU | Colorado State University

artifacts.claimText: The CSU System AI Governance Guidelines state that they apply to individuals handling institutional data or using AI tools in administrative operations, research, clinical, and educational activities across the CSU system.

For CSU System internal, confidential, and restricted data classifications, the AI Governance Guidelines say users should verify data classification, follow applicable policies, and use CSU-approved tools; restricted data also requires additional approval for AI use.
CSU TILT guidance says AI syllabus statements should remain consistent with university and department policy on assessing student work and provide students clarity about instructor expectations.

citation.citationTitle: What should a syllabus statement on AI look like? | The Institute for Learning and Teaching
citation.publisher: Colorado State University The Institute for Learning and Teaching

artifacts.claimText: CSU's Student Resolution Center defines cheating to include unauthorized sources or assistance and instructor-prohibited behavior, and defines plagiarism as representing another's language, structure, images, ideas, or thoughts as one's own without proper acknowledgment.

citation.publisher: Colorado State University Student Resolution Center

artifacts.claimText: An official CSU MTI teaching page states that work submitted for credit that was created by AI engines can be addressed under multiple areas of the Academic Misconduct section of the Student Conduct Code.

citation.citationTitle: AI Syllabus Statements & the CSU Conduct Code
citation.publisher: Colorado State University College of Natural Sciences

artifacts.sourceTitle: AI Tools | AI @ CSU | Colorado State University
artifacts.evidenceSnippetOriginal: Only three tools are currently approved for handling sensitive CSU data (such as research data, student information, or protected university records): CSU-GPT; Microsoft Copilot Chat (When logged in with your CSU NetID); Microsoft Teams Premium.

citation.citationTitle: AI Tools | AI @ CSU | Colorado State University

artifacts.sourceTitle: AI Tools | AI @ CSU | Colorado State University
artifacts.evidenceSnippetOriginal: You may use other tools for non-sensitive, public information only. Many commercial AI tools have privacy statements that allow them to collect and store your data, and in many cases, they may use your input to train future models.
artifacts.evidenceLocator: Can I use other AI tools (like ChatGPT, Gemini, or Anthropic Claude)?

citation.citationTitle: AI Tools | AI @ CSU | Colorado State University

artifacts.sourceTitle: CSU-GPT | AI @ CSU | Colorado State University
artifacts.evidenceSnippetOriginal: Unlike public AI tools, CSU-GPT keeps all data, prompts, and uploads inside the university's Microsoft Azure tenant. That means your data never leaves CSU.
artifacts.evidenceLocator: A Smarter, Safer AI for CSU

citation.citationTitle: CSU-GPT | AI @ CSU | Colorado State University

artifacts.sourceTitle: AI Governance Guidelines | Data Governance
artifacts.evidenceSnippetOriginal: These guidelines apply to all individuals handling institutional data or using AI tools in administrative operations, research, clinical, and educational activities across the CSU system.

artifacts.sourceTitle: AI Governance Guidelines | Data Governance
artifacts.evidenceSnippetOriginal: Level 2 (Internal): Verify data classification before use, adhere to approved CSU data and AI policies, use only CSU-approved tools... Level 4 (Restricted): Verify data classification before use... use only CSU-approved tools with the highest level of contractual data protection guarantees; requires additional approval for any AI use.

artifacts.sourceTitle: What should a syllabus statement on AI look like? | The Institute for Learning and Teaching
artifacts.evidenceSnippetOriginal: However, it is important that they remain consistent with university and department policy on assessing student work. Finally, the most important part of including a statement is that it provides clarity to your students about your expectations.

citation.citationTitle: What should a syllabus statement on AI look like? | The Institute for Learning and Teaching
citation.publisher: Colorado State University The Institute for Learning and Teaching

artifacts.sourceTitle: Academic Misconduct - Student Resolution Center
artifacts.evidenceSnippetOriginal: Cheating includes using unauthorized sources of information and providing or receiving unauthorized assistance on any form of academic work or engaging in any behavior specifically prohibited by the instructor in the course syllabus or class presentation. Plagiarism includes the copying of language, structure, images, ideas, or thoughts of another, and representing them as one's own without proper acknowledgment.

citation.publisher: Colorado State University Student Resolution Center

artifacts.sourceTitle: AI Syllabus Statements & the CSU Conduct Code
artifacts.evidenceSnippetOriginal: Is work (essays, responses, code, images) created by an artificial intelligence engine still covered by our Student Conduct Code's language? Yes. Definitively. The Student Conduct Code was written to address behavior, not technologies. In addition, work submitted for credit that was created by AI-engines can be addressed using multiple areas of the Academic Misconduct section of the Student Conduct Code.
artifacts.evidenceLocator: AI and the CSU Student Conduct Code

citation.citationTitle: AI Syllabus Statements & the CSU Conduct Code
citation.publisher: Colorado State University College of Natural Sciences

artifacts.notes: Approved as a narrow claim because the official AI Tools page directly lists the currently approved tools for sensitive CSU data.
Approved because the evidence explicitly limits other commercial AI tools to non-sensitive public information.
Approved because the CSU-GPT page directly describes the Azure tenant data handling claim.
Approved because the scope statement is quoted directly from the CSU System AI Governance Guidelines.
Approved because the data-classification AI requirements are explicit in the governance source.
Approved as teaching guidance, not as a binding university-wide student AI policy.
Approved because the Student Resolution Center page directly defines cheating and plagiarism categories.
Approved as narrow MTI teaching guidance; it is not treated as a standalone central policy.

artifacts.title: Colorado State University AI Policy Source Review
artifacts.summary: Staged official-source crawl for Colorado State University found official AI at CSU pages for approved tools and CSU-GPT, CSU System AI Governance Guidelines for institutional data and responsible AI use, TILT guidance for AI syllabus statements, and Student Resolution Center academic misconduct definitions. Claims are scoped to these official pages; department, publication, bootcamp, and stale PDF sources were rejected or kept out of central-policy claims.
limitations: This tracker is not legal advice, not academic integrity advice, and not an official university statement unless a linked source is the university's own official page.
The staged bundle does not assert a single finalized central student AI coursework policy; teaching claims are scoped to TILT and MTI guidance.
Search did not find central official research-specific generative AI policy beyond CSU System data governance and existing research-review references.
artifacts.sourceRightsPolicy: Tracker metadata is open licensed. Official source documents, page text, PDFs, and other source materials retain their original rights and terms.
